elements.sent-2411-004: Box 9513, 2300 RA Leiden, The Netherlands \and Max Planck Institute for Astronomy, K\"onigstuhl 17, D-69117 Heidelberg, Germany \and JWST Australian Data Centre, Centre for Astrophysics and Supercomputing, Swinburne University of Technology, Victoria 3122, Australia \and Institut de Recherche en Astrophysique et Planétologie (IRAP), Université de Toulouse, CNRS, UPS, CNES, 31400 Toulouse, France \abstract { The Hydrogen Lyman-alpha (\Lya) line, the brightest rest-frame UV-line of high-redshift galaxies, shows a large variety of shapes which is caused by factors at different scales, from the interstellar medium to the intergalactic medium (IGM).}
elements.sent-2411-005: We develop a method to classify \Lya\ emission lines in four spectral and three spatial categories, by combining a pure spectral analysis with a narrow-band image analysis.
elements.sent-2411-006: We measure spectral properties, such as the peak separation and the blue-to-total flux ratio (B/T) for the double-peaked galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our analysis reveals that between 32\% and 51\% of the galaxies exhibit double-peaked profiles, with peak separations ranging from 150 km~s$^{-1}$ to nearly 1600 km~s$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-008: The fraction of double-peaks seems to evolve dependently with the \Lya\ luminosity, while we don't notice a severe decrease of this fraction with redshift, as expected due to the IGM attenuation at high reshift.
elements.sent-2411-009: An artificial increase of the number of double-peaks at the highest redshifts may cause the observation of a plateau instead of a decrease.
elements.sent-2411-010: A notable amount of these double-peaked profiles shows blue-dominated spectra, suggesting unique gas dynamics and inflow characteristics in some high-redshift galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-011: The consequent fraction of blue-dominated spectra needs to be confirmed by obtaining new systemic redshift measurements.
elements.sent-2411-012: Among the double-peaked galaxies, 4\% are spurious detections, i.e. the blue and red peaks do not come from the same spatial location.
elements.sent-2411-013: Around 20\% out of the 477 sources of the parent sample lie in a complex environment, meaning there are other clumps or galaxies at the same redshift within a distance of 30 kpc.
elements.sent-2411-014: We also need more data to confirm the trend seen at low redshift.
elements.sent-2411-015: In addition, it is crucial to obtain secure systemic redshifts for LAEs to better constrain the nature of the double-peaks.
elements.sent-2411-016: Statistical samples of double-peaks and triple-peaks are a promising probe of the evolution of the physical properties of galaxies across cosmic time.
elements.sent-2411-017: The Lyman-alpha (\Lya, $\lambda 1216$ \AA) line of Hydrogen, as the brightest UV-line of star-forming galaxies \citep{PartridgePeebles1967 , is a key spectral feature in the observation of high-redshift galaxies, and often the only detected signal \citep[e.g.,][]{rhoads2004,malhotra2004,Maseda2018 .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: A remarkable characteristic of this line is the wide diversity of spectral shapes that have been reported in the literature, at every redshift \citep[e.g.,][]{Kulas2012,henry2015,Yang2016,leclercq2017,kerutt2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The archetypical \Lya\ shape, making it easily identifiable, is a single red asymmetric line profile \citep[e.g.,][]{Shapley2003, Tapken2007 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: But over the last decade, double-peaked \Lya\ lines \citep{henry2015,hu2016, matthee2018, Songaila2018, meyer2021, hayes2021 as well as triple-peaked ones \citep[e.g.][]{vanzella2016,naidu2017,vanzella2018,Izotov2018b,rivera-thorsen2019 have been observed.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The observation of this wide diversity of line shapes has become possible thanks to the emergence of instruments such as the Cosmic Origins Spectrograph onboard the Hubble Space Telescope \citep[HST/COS,][]{Green2012 which observed low-$z$ \Lya\ emitting galaxies, the Multi-Unit Spectroscopic Explorer at the Very Large Telescope \citep[VLT/MUSE,][]{Bacon2010 , which has unveiled a large population of faint star-forming galaxies at $z = 3-6$ \citep{bacon2023 , and the Near Infrared Spectrograph onboard the James Webb Space Telescope (JWST/NIRSpec), which is already pushing the limits of \Lya\ line observation towards higher redshifts \citep{Bunker2023 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The wavelength coverage of these instruments, ranging from UV to IR, enables the scientific community to observe the evolution of galaxies over a time span of approximately 13 Gyr, using the same indicator: the \Lya\ line.
elements.sent-2411-023: The observed diversity of spectral shapes arises from the resonant nature of the \Lya\ line \citep[see e.g.,][for a review on \Lya\ radiation transfer effects in galaxies]{dijkstra2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Therefore, depending on the sightline, the shape of the \Lya\ line profile may vary drastically \citep{blaizot2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The observed \Lya\ line profiles encode information on the gas velocity and its density distribution as the \Lya\ photons travel through the interstellar medium (ISM).
elements.sent-2411-026: For double-peaks in particular, \cite{verhamme2015 suggest that the separation between the two peaks of the \Lya\ line correlates with the neutral Hydrogen column density.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Indeed, a relationship between the \Lya\ peak separation and the Lyman-continuum (LyC) escape fraction has been found empirically for low-$z$ LyC leakers \citep{Verhamme2017, Izotov2021, Flury2022 and has been used at higher redshift on samples of \Lya\ emitting galaxies (LAEs) to select LyC leaking candidates \citep{naidu2022, kramarenko2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Nevertheless, \cite{kerutt2024 could not confirm this relationship with their LAE sample at $z = 3-4$.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The value of the blue-to-total flux ratio (B/T), another spectral property containing physical information, characterises the gas exchanges between the galaxy and its surrounding environment.
elements.sent-2411-030: In simulations, expanding shells produce red-dominated \Lya\ lines \citep[i.e. B/T < 0.5,][]{Verhamme2006 , whereas blue-dominated spectra are seen when gas falls into the galaxy \citep{blaizot2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: The brightest \Lya\ phases of galaxies seem to be outflow phases \citep{blaizot2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: In observations, more red-dominated spectra have been observed than blue-dominated ones \citep{Kulas2012, trainor2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: A detailed analysis has only been done for a few blue-dominated objects \citep{mukherjee2023, furtak2022, marques-chaves2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Finally, the intergalactic Medium (IGM) transmission decreases with increasing redshift, preferentially suppressing flux on the blue part of the \Lya\ line \citep{Laursen2011, Garel2021, hayes2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: To quantify the inflow/outflow phases, constrain the duty cycle of galaxies and to study LyC leakers across different redshifts, it is important to measure the peak separation and the B/T flux ratio for a large number of galaxies, over a large redshift range.
elements.sent-2411-036: Until now, the diversity of the \Lya\ line profile has been quantified by dedicated surveys targeting previously detected LAEs.
elements.sent-2411-037: The way the double-peaks are identified among a population of LAEs introduces biases.
elements.sent-2411-038: Indeed, most of the time the double-peaks are visually identified \citep[e.g.][]{sobral2018 or a combination of a detection algorithm and a visual inspection is used \citep{Kulas2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In this study we use for the first time an automatic algorithm on a blind survey without pre-selection, to quantify the diversity of the \Lya\ lines, both spectrally and spatially.
elements.sent-2411-040: This paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-041: In Sect.~\ref{sec:2 , we describe the MUSE-Deep data and the sample selection.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Sect.~\ref{sec:3 is dedicated to the description of the method developed for identifying and characterising the spectral profiles of the \Lya\ lines of our sample of galaxies.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In Sect.~\ref{sec:4 , we present the results of the classification and the universal fraction of double-peaks.}
elements.sent-2411-044: The results are discussed in Sect.~\ref{sec:5 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: Finally, the summary and conclusions are given in Sect.~\ref{sec:6 .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Throughout this paper, we assume a $\Lambda$CDM cosmology with $\Omega_{m}$=0.3, $\Omega_{\Lambda}$=0.7, and H$_{0}$=67.4 km~s$^{-1}$ Mpc$^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-047: We constructed a sample of $z$-selected galaxies from the Data Release 2 (DR2) catalogue \citep[hereafter B23]{bacon2023 \defcitealias{bacon2023 {B23 of the MUSE eXtremely Deep Field (hereafter \mxdf), to identify and characterise their \Lya\ emission line $\lambda 1216$ \AA\ based on publicly available spectra\footnote{\url{https://amused.univ-lyon1.fr/ .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Our data set is described in Sect.~\ref{sec:21 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Sect.~\ref{sec:22 is dedicated to our sample selection and finally, the choice of spectral extraction is discussed in Sect.~\ref{sec:23 .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: The \mxdf\ data were taken as part of the MUSE guaranteed time observations (GTO) program between August 2018 and January 2019.
elements.sent-2411-051: All observations were performed using the MUSE ground-layer adaptive optics (GLAO) mode.
elements.sent-2411-052: Details about the \mxdf\ data processing and the production of the source catalogues can be found in the survey paper \citetalias{bacon2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: In summary, a single pointing with the deepest exposure time of 140 hours was achieved.
elements.sent-2411-054: The final \mxdf\ field of view has a circular shape designed to minimise systematics, with the following centre coordinates: 53\textdegree.16467, -27\textdegree.78537 (J2000 FK5).
elements.sent-2411-055: The exposure time of the field exceeds 100 hours in the inner 31\arcsec\ radius, which represents an area of 0.84 arcmin$^{2}$, while it reaches around 10 hours at a radius of 41\arcsec\ (area of 1.47 arcmin$^{2}$, Fig.~\ref{fig:expmap ).}
elements.sent-2411-056: The 50\% \Lya\ detection completeness, in the deepest 140-hour area, is achieved for an AB magnitude of 28.7 (F775W) at $z = 3.2 - 4.5$.
elements.sent-2411-057: The field overlaps partially with the other MUSE Hubble Ultra Deep Fields \citep[HUDF,][]{Bacon2017 of 31-hour (1 $\times$ 1 arcmin$^{2}$) and 10-hour (3 $\times$ 3 arcmin$^{2}$) total exposure times, called \udft\ and \mosaic, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The 50\% completeness is reached at 26.5 mag and 25.5 mag ($z \sim 4$) in HST F775W, in \udft\ and \mosaic, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-059: The \mxdf\ is two orders of magnitude deeper than \udft.
elements.sent-2411-060: The \mxdf\ data cover a wavelength range from 4700 \AA\ to 9350 \AA\, excluding an adaptive optics (AO) gap from 5800 \AA\ to 5966.25 \AA\ due to the notch filter that blocks the bright light from the four sodium laser guide stars.
elements.sent-2411-061: Both the \udft\ and \mosaic\ fields were observed without AO and therefore do not have any AO gap in wavelength.
elements.sent-2411-062: In the \mxdf, the full-width at half maximum (FWHM) of the Moffat point spread function (Moffat PSF, \citeauthor{Moffat1969 \citeyear{Moffat1969 ) is 0.6\arcsec\ at 4700 \AA\ and 0.4\arcsec\ at 9350 \AA.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: The line spread function (LSF) of the \mxdf\ is constant in the field and larger in the outer parts.
elements.sent-2411-064: The mean \mxdf\ LSF over the wavelength range is 2.6 \AA\ which corresponds to a LSF of $\approx$ 150 km~s$^{-1}$ at $z = 3$ and $\approx$ 90 km~s$^{-1}$ at $z = 6$ (see details in Sect.~4.2.2 of \citetalias{bacon2023 ).}
elements.sent-2411-065: The data reduction process performed on the DR2 survey is similar to the one for the Data Release 1 survey and is described in \cite{Bacon2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-066: However, some improvements have been made, especially in the sky-subtraction process (Appendix~B of \citetalias{bacon2023 ).}
elements.sent-2411-067: The output data of the \mxdf\ is a 3D datacube with dimensions of 3721 $\times$ 470 $\times$ 470 pixels, meaning that for each spatial pixel of $0\farcs2 \, \times \, 0\farcs2$, a spectrum divided into 3721 pixels of 1.25~\AA\ width is available.
elements.sent-2411-068: On top of the signal datacube, a variance datacube is provided.

elements.sent-2411-069: The DR2 catalogue compiles all sources observed in the three MUSE Ultra Deep Fields: \mosaic\ (9 arcmin$^{2}$), \udft\ (1 arcmin$^{2}$) and \mxdf\ (1.47 arcmin$^{2}$) (see Fig.~2 of \citetalias{bacon2023 ).}
elements.sent-2411-070: This catalogue contains 2221 sources, from nearby galaxies ($z < 0.25$) to high-redshift galaxies (up to $z$ $\approx$ $6.64$).
elements.sent-2411-071: The \Lya\ line is observable by MUSE in the redshift range $z = 2.87 - 6.64$.

elements.sent-2411-076: The first step in building our parent sample consists of selecting the objects detected in the 1.47 arcmin$^{2}$ \mxdf\ field of view, with a spectroscopic redshift above 2.87.

elements.sent-2411-109: We will now investigate and describe the diversity of the \Lya\ profiles among our parent sample of 477 objects.
elements.sent-2411-110: When exploring the spectral properties of the \Lya\ emitters observed with MUSE, we noticed the wide diversity of profiles in the \Lya\ line.
elements.sent-2411-111: We thus developed a method aiming at characterising their spectral profiles.
elements.sent-2411-112: This section is dedicated to its step-by-step description.
elements.sent-2411-113: An overview of the method can be seen in Fig.~\ref{fig:flowchart .}

elements.sent-2411-114: Indeed, previous studies have shown multi-peaked \Lya\ lines with peak separations larger than 1000 km~s$^{-1}$ \citep{kerutt2022 , even reaching values around 1800 km~s$^{-1}$ \citep{Kulas2012 .}}
elements.sent-2411-115: We use the following formula to convert wavelengths into velocities (in km~s$^{-1}$): \begin{equation}\rm V = \frac{\rm \lambda}{(1+z)\times1215.67 \, \text{\AA}} \times \frac{c}{1000},\end{equation} where $\lambda$ is the wavelength in vacuum in \AA, $z$ is the redshift given by the \Lya\ wavelength in the DR2 catalogue and $c$ is the speed of light in vacuum in m~s$^{-1}$ given by astropy.constants\footnote{\url{https://docs.astropy.org/en/stable/constants/index.html .}}
elements.sent-2411-116: Before using this equation, our data are corrected using the air-to-vacuum correction function\footnote{\url{https://mpdaf.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/mpdaf.obj.vactoair.html\#mpdaf.obj.vactoair of the Python package MPDAF \citep[MUSE Python Data Analysis Framework,][]{piqueras2019 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: Among the 477 sources of the parent sample, fifteen have their \Lya\ line located near a MUSE wavelength edge, which limits the information we have to correctly process them:.
elements.sent-2411-118: We still run the classification on these 15 objects, but we flag them as potentially missing information for reliable classification (Sect.~\ref{sec:413 ).}
elements.sent-2411-119: The extracted spectra provided by the \origin, \odhin\ or \nbext\ methods are not continuum subtracted and some sources show a stellar continuum.
elements.sent-2411-120: To accurately analyse the \Lya\ emission line using our classification method, we subtract the continuum following the protocol used in \cite{kusakabe2022 adapted to the DR2 data (top left black box in Fig.~\ref{fig:flowchart ).}}
elements.sent-2411-121: In brief, the continuum spectra are estimated from spectral median filtering of the original spectra in a 100-pixel spectral window.
elements.sent-2411-122: At the end of this step, we have built a continuum-subtracted rest-frame spectrum, centred on the DR2 spectroscopic redshift, over the same rest-frame wavelength window, for all 477 objects of the parent sample.
elements.sent-2411-123: For each source, we generate 100 realizations of the 1D-extracted spectrum where the flux value of each pixel is randomly drawn from a normal distribution centred on its original flux value with a standard deviation given by its variance.
elements.sent-2411-124: Then, we determine their S/N spectra by dividing the flux by the square root of the variance provided by the variance datacube.
elements.sent-2411-125: For each of the one hundred S/N spectra, a peak is detected when the S/N value per pixel is above 1 for at least 2 adjacent pixels.
elements.sent-2411-126: Assuming that the noise of adjacent pixels is not correlated, the false-positive detection rate for an S/N = 2 pixel or for 2 adjacent pixels with an SN = 1 is similar ($\sim$ 2.3\% and $\sim$ 2.5\%).
elements.sent-2411-127: We choose to use a criterion of SN $\geq$ 1 for at least 2 adjacent pixels to avoid noise spikes of 1-pixel width and to allow the detection of faint (S/N $\approx$ 2) \Lya\ emission.
elements.sent-2411-128: For each pixel of the 100 generated spectra, a value of 1 is assigned if this pixel belongs to a peak, otherwise, a value of 0 is set.
elements.sent-2411-129: For every generated spectrum, we thus have a list of 0 and 1.
elements.sent-2411-130: We then sum these lists for the 100 spectra and we obtain a single list with values between 0 and 100, resulting in the detection spectrum (see solid line in panel (a) of Fig.~\ref{fig:ID3240_GOLD ).}
elements.sent-2411-131: The detection spectrum reaches 100 when a pixel belongs to a peak in 100\% of the generated spectra and 0 when a pixel never belongs to a peak.
elements.sent-2411-132: Finally, to select the final areas of signal, we apply a detection threshold of N = 40 on those detection spectra (solid spectrum above the horizontal dashed green line in panel (a) of Fig.~\ref{fig:ID3240_GOLD ) and each pixel above this threshold is considered as real signal (shaded areas in panel (a) of Fig.~\ref{fig:ID3240_GOLD ).}}
elements.sent-2411-133: The value of N = 40 has been chosen empirically and is discussed in App.~\ref{ap:A .}
elements.sent-2411-134: The number of peaks of each \Lya\ line corresponds to the number of areas of signal.
elements.sent-2411-135: In certain instances, an area of signal might encompass a double-peak with some flux present in the trough between the peaks.
elements.sent-2411-136: Consequently, the detection spectrum in the signal area does not fall below N = 40 (for example see Fig.~\ref{fig:ID399_satellite in Sect.~\ref{sec:51 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-137: To detect such double-peaks, we conduct a flux variation analysis.
elements.sent-2411-138: This analysis consists in comparing the flux value pixel per pixel, starting from the highest value of the area of signal, i.e. the peak of the line.
elements.sent-2411-139: The method analyses the flux variation on both sides of the peak until reaching the edges of the area of signal.
elements.sent-2411-140: If the flux value of the pixel $n+1$ is higher than the value of the pixel $n$, then a secondary peak is detected by the method, as illustrated in panel (a) of Fig.~\ref{fig:ID399_satellite .}

elements.subsection-2411-009: Test of the method on background spectra
elements.sent-2411-187: In order to test the reliability of the classification method on the detection of noise spikes as signal, we applied the method on 100 spectra at $z = 3$ and 100 at $z = 6$.
elements.sent-2411-188: We selected 100 random places in the continuum-subtracted \mxdf\ cube and extracted the spectra in a 2 arcseconds diameter circular aperture.
elements.sent-2411-189: We selected two zones on each spectrum, one zone of $\pm$ 2000 km~s$^{-1}$ window around the \Lya\ line peak wavelength as if it were emitted at $z = 3$, and another one as if the \Lya\ line peaked at $z = 6$.
elements.sent-2411-190: We then reproduced the exact same procedure described in Sect.~\ref{sec:32 and Sect.~\ref{sec:33 .}}
elements.sent-2411-191: A summary of the results is given in Table~\ref{table:bkg_spectra .}
elements.sent-2411-192: At $z = 3$, over the whole selection of background spectra, 91 of them do not have any peak detected by the method.
elements.sent-2411-193: First, 52 do not pass the first part of the method (Sect.~\ref{sec:32 ).}
elements.sent-2411-194: No peaks are detected on the detection spectra.
elements.sent-2411-195: For the remaining 48 background spectra, 12 of them are discarded throughout the NB image verification (Sect.~\ref{sec:33 ).}
elements.sent-2411-196: Finally, the last step of extracting the spectra of the SourceFinder segmentation maps enables to eliminate 27 background spectra.
elements.sent-2411-197: Only 6 background spectra remain with a noise peak detected, 1 with two noise peaks.
elements.sent-2411-198: One background spectrum is clearly contaminated by a neighbouring galaxy and one last spectrum presents a clear emission line (the SNR of the peak of the line peaks above 5).
elements.sent-2411-199: In total, the method detects noise peaks in 7\% of the background spectra at $z = 3$.
elements.sent-2411-200: At $z = 6$, over the whole selection of background spectra, 87 of them do not have any peak detected by the method.

elements.sent-2411-203: Finally, the last step of extracting the spectra of the SourceFinder segmentation maps enables to eliminate 47 background spectra.

elements.sent-2411-216: The first part of this section is devoted to the definition of an unbiased sample, cleaned from observational limitations.

elements.sent-2411-403: The \Lya\ line is resonant, and radiative transfer effects can result in a double-peaked profile, with peaks on each side of the resonance frequency \citep{neufeld1990, dijkstra2006, dijkstra2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-404: Based on this assumption, methods have been developed to retrieve the systemic redshift of a galaxy using the \Lya\ line shape \citep{verhamme2018 , or the escape fraction of the ionising radiation using the \Lya\ peak separation \citep{verhamme2015 .}}
elements.sent-2411-405: Moreover, the B/T measurements can provide information about the gas kinematic configuration (inflows/outflows), as demonstrated in \cite{blaizot2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-406: Throughout this study, we call "blue peak" the peak with the shortest wavelength and "red peak" the one at the longer wavelength, although we do not know the systemic redshift of most of our galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-407: Our population of double-peaked LAEs may differ from what is usually called a double-peak in the sense that the blue (red) peak might not be bluer (redder) than the resonant frequency of the central object.
elements.sent-2411-408: In the same way, single-peaked lines are by default presumed being on the red side of the resonant frequency, since we do not know their systemics redshift.
elements.sent-2411-409: But this assumption for single-peaked lines does not impact the results presented in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-410: In this section, we investigate the spectral parameters of a subsample of galaxies with known systemic redshift whose spectra are shown in App.~\ref{ap:F .}
elements.sent-2411-411: Fifteen objects have a secure systemic redshift measured from nebular lines detected in their MUSE spectra.

elements.sent-2411-414: One last (ID 6666) has its redshift measured by VLT/KMOS in \cite{boogaard2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-415: Eight sources of this subsample with systemic redshift are double-peaked, three are single-peaked and the last four are triple-peaked.
elements.sent-2411-416: They all belong in the \gold\ category.
elements.sent-2411-417: The luminosities of those galaxies do not go lower than log(L$_{Ly\alpha}$ [erg s$^{-1}$]) = 40 but they populate the bright end of the distribution with a mean logarithmic \Lya\ luminosity of 42.25 (panel a).

elements.sent-2411-445: In this paper, we presented the diversity of the \Lya\ line shapes among a population of MUSE LAEs.
elements.sent-2411-446: We constructed a parent sample of 477 redshift-selected ($z = 2.87 - 6.64$) sources from a blind, extremely deep, MUSE GTO survey, the \mxdf\ \citep[up to 140 hours of integration time,][]{bacon2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-447: The main results of our work are summarised as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-448: We developed a method to detect and characterise the \Lya\ emission lines in both spectral and spatial dimensions (Sect.~\ref{sec:3 ).}
elements.sent-2411-449: The \Lya\ lines from the sources of the parent sample are quantitatively classified into four different spectral categories: no-peak (9), single-peak (198), double-peak (248) and triple-peak (22), see Sect.~\ref{sec:411 and Fig.~\ref{fig:ex_lya_line .}}
elements.sent-2411-450: The analysis of the spatial distribution of the \Lya\ peaks lead to the division of the parent sample into three different categories: \gold, \silver\ and \bronze\ (Sect.~\ref{sec:34 and Fig~\ref{fig:categories_G_S_B ).}}
elements.sent-2411-451: An object is classified as \gold\ if all the peaks originate from the same spatial location.
elements.sent-2411-452: When the peak emission arises from several distinct regions contained within the reference segmentation map, the object is thus classified as a \silver\ object.
elements.sent-2411-453: Finally, for double- and triple-peaks, an object can be classified \bronze.
elements.sent-2411-454: In that case, each peak has an emission coming from a distinct spatial location, which means the peaks are coming from different sources.
elements.sent-2411-455: Among the 248 double-peaks, 238 are \gold\ or \silver\ objects (i.e. considered as double-peaks coming from radiative transfer processes).
elements.sent-2411-456: The last ten cannot be considered as such given their spatial distribution (i.e. \bronze\ objects).
elements.sent-2411-457: We identified several observational limitations: the fainter the source, the harder it is to detect multi-peaks; the minimum peak separation detectable varies with redshift; the detectable range of B/T ratios depends on the S/N (see Sect.~\ref{sec:412 ).}
elements.sent-2411-458: Taking into account these limitations, we built an unbiased sample of 206 galaxies, with \Lya\ luminosities above 3 $\times$ 10$^{40}$ erg~s$^{-1}$, peak separations larger than 150 km~s$^{-1}$ and S/N above 7.
elements.sent-2411-459: For each object, we measured spectral properties such as the \Lya\ flux, the \Lya\ luminosity, the S/N, the FWHM, and the peak separation and blue-to-total flux ratio (B/T) for double-peaks.
elements.sent-2411-460: Our study reaches fainter luminosities (log(L$_{Ly\alpha}$ [erg s$^{-1}$]) = 39.5) thanks to the depth of our data.
elements.sent-2411-461: Our derived FWHM and the peak separation values from the unbiased sample are comparable to other studies in the literature, whereas the B/T distribution shows an important fraction of of blue-dominated spectra (Sect.~\ref{sec:423 ).}
elements.sent-2411-462: We found a fraction of double-peaks X$_{\rm DP}$ oscillating between 32\% and 51\%, obtained by two double-peak unbiased samples, one restrictive giving the lower limit and one inclusive giving the upper limit (Sect.~\ref{sec:431 ).}
elements.sent-2411-463: This fraction seems to evolve dependently with the \Lya\ luminosity (Sect.~\ref{sec:432 ).}
elements.sent-2411-464: This fraction of double-peaks shows a global decrease with redshift, stagnating at high redshift (Sect.~\ref{sec:433 ).}
elements.sent-2411-465: This plateau might be due to the high mean \Lya\ luminosity of each redshift bin, knowing that brighter galaxies seem to have more double-peaked \Lya\ lines.
elements.sent-2411-466: Nevertheless, if the increase of the X$_{\rm DP}$ at lower redshift is confirmed with more data, the use of the double-peak fraction to probe the opacity of the IGM becomes less pertinent.
elements.sent-2411-467: The double-peaks identified by the method are not necessarily double-peaks originating from radiative transfer (\bronze\ category, see Sect.~\ref{sec:51 ), but could come from satellites.}
elements.sent-2411-468: In fact, the NB images of the \Lya\ peaks detected by the method show a wide variety of environments for galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-469: This method unveiled the detection of satellites (companions) of main targeted galaxies and also interactions between galaxies (pairs, triplet systems), independently of the spectral category (Sect.~\ref{sec:52 ).}
elements.sent-2411-470: A detailed characterisation of the environment of our pairs and triplet galaxies will be the subject of an upcoming study (Vitte et al. in prep.).
elements.sent-2411-471: Peak separation and B/T flux ratio have a physical meaning (i.e. they are tracers of the escape of ionising photons for peak separation < 400 km~s$^{-1}$ and infalling gas for B/T > 0.5) only if the systemic velocity is located between the blue and the red peaks.
elements.sent-2411-472: We were able to measure a systemic redshift for a subsample of 15 galaxies from the MUSE data (Sect.~\ref{sec:53 ).}
elements.sent-2411-473: The only blue dominant peak having a systemic redshift has its two peaks located on the red side of the systemic redshift.
elements.sent-2411-474: This spectrum thus can not be considered as tracing infalling gas.
elements.sent-2411-475: More systemic redshift measurements are needed in order to interpret our distributions further.
elements.sent-2411-476: A recently approved KMOS proposal (113.2682, PI: Vitte, 60hrs) will allow us to increase this subsample of systemic redshifts.
elements.sent-2411-477: This paper aims at describing the method we developed to classify \Lya\ emission lines from high-redshift galaxies and its use on a blind sample from MUSE GTO surveys.
elements.sent-2411-478: A more detailed study of the completeness will be the scope of a future work.
elements.sent-2411-479: We plan to apply the method developed and used for this work to other data sets which will give us a more general view on the fraction of double-peaks among LAEs and how it evolves with redshift.
elements.sent-2411-480: We will also use existing data \citep[e.g. FRESCO, JADES,][respectively]{oesch2023, Eisenstein2023 and obtain new data (KMOS proposal, 113.2682, PI: Vitte, 60hrs) in order to get the systemic redshift of more galaxies of the \mxdf\ to constrain the distributions we found in this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-481: The infrared instruments such as VLT/KMOS and JWST/NIRSpec allow us to perform a multi-wavelength study to explore the possible physical peculiarities of the different categories of LAEs.
elements.sent-2411-482: In the longer term, this work can be pursued at lower redshift, e.g. down to $z \approx 2$ thanks to the upcoming BlueMUSE \citep{Richard2019 , allowing us to unveil the evolution of the diversity of the LAE population with time, over 3 more Gyr of galaxy evolution.. \bibliographystyle{aa.}}

elements.sent-2411-489: Threshold determination for detection spectra To disentangle the \Lya\ emission peaks from the noise spikes, a detection threshold had to be chosen to apply to detection spectra (Fig.~\ref{fig:ID3240_GOLD ).}
elements.sent-2411-490: We chose a threshold of N = 40, meaning that a pixel is assigned as containing \Lya\ emission if it has been at least detected in 40 out of the 100 generated spectra (explained in detail in Sect.~\ref{sec:32 ).}
elements.sent-2411-491: It is an empirical value based on the data used in this work.
elements.sent-2411-492: To test this threshold, we also performed the classification method with a threshold of N = 30 and N = 50.
elements.sent-2411-493: The corresponding classification results, before and after the NB image verification, are given in Table~\ref{table:thresholds_spectra_before_photutils and Table~\ref{table:thresholds_spectra_after_photutils , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-494: In Table~\ref{table:thresholds_spectra_before_photutils , we notice strong differences between the fraction of the different line shapes.}
elements.sent-2411-495: The number of single-peaks more than doubles between both extreme threshold values while the fraction of triple-peaks increases by five between a threshold of N = 50 and N = 30.
elements.sent-2411-496: The NB image verification done in Sect.~\ref{sec:33 tends to homogenise the fractions between the thresholds, especially between N = 30 and N = 40, as seen in Table~\ref{table:thresholds_spectra_after_photutils .}}
elements.sent-2411-497: It appears that a threshold of N = 30 selects noise peaks on the spectra, which is well seen in the fraction of triple-peaks and double-peaks of Table~\ref{table:thresholds_spectra_before_photutils and the NB image verification performed cleans these noise peaks to reach fraction values similar to the ones of the N = 40 threshold.}
elements.sent-2411-498: On the contrary, a threshold of N = 50 is too restrictive and tends to discard small bumps, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:ID7299_threshold50 .}
elements.sent-2411-499: Indeed, the area of signal B would be discarded with a threshold of N = 50 (red dashed line in panel (a) of Fig.~\ref{fig:ID7299_threshold50 ).}
elements.sent-2411-500: Specifically, the NB image corresponding to the area of signal B displayed panel (e) of Fig.~\ref{fig:ID7299_threshold50 shows SourceFinder detection at the centre with a significant orange extracted spectrum (see Sect.~\ref{sec:33 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-501: Finally, we measured spectral parameters on the \Lya\ lines with the different thresholds.
elements.sent-2411-502: With a threshold of N = 30, we tend to measure more flux but with lower S/N, which is expected as we increase the width of the areas of signal.
elements.sent-2411-503: With a threshold of N = 50, the spectral measurements are very similar to the ones at N = 40.
elements.sent-2411-504: The values measured at N = 30 and N = 50 are inside the errorbars of the measurements done with the N = 40 threshold.
elements.sent-2411-505: As a result of these tests, a detection threshold of N = 40 has been chosen.

elements.sent-2411-506: Threshold determination on NB images In order to detect emission on NBs, a threshold of 2$\sigma$ on three connected pixels has been chosen (Sect.~\ref{sec:33 ).}
elements.sent-2411-507: This choice has been made for two reasons.
elements.sent-2411-508: The first reason is to be coherent with the criterion used to detect areas of signal on the spectrum (Sect.~\ref{sec:32 ): we used a minimum S/N of 1 per pixel for at least two adjacent pixels, which is similar to only one pixel with a S/N of 2.}
elements.sent-2411-509: The second reason why we did not choose more strict parameters to avoid false detections, like the ones in Fig.~\ref{fig:ID3240_GOLD (detections labeled 0, 2 and 3), is to be able to detect compact and faint objects such as in the \bronze\ category.}
elements.sent-2411-510: Using a threshold of 2$\sigma$ on the NB is thus a valid choice as we want to be consistent and inclusive.
elements.sent-2411-511: We tested two different configurations on the parent sample: without the deblending function and with the default parameters of the deblending function\footnote{\url{https://photutils.readthedocs.io/en/stable/api/photutils.segmentation.SourceFinder.html\#photutils.segmentation.SourceFinder of SourceFinder.}}
elements.sent-2411-512: While not using the deblending function, emission from other galaxies present on the NB image and close enough to our source is blended with the actual emission of our source.
elements.sent-2411-513: When looking at the same galaxies with the deblending function on, in this configuration, the different emissions are well differentiated.
elements.sent-2411-514: For this study, we choose to use the deblending function of \photutils.SourceFinder, which will differentiate sources with a 7.5 magnitude difference (contrast=0.001, default value).

elements.section-2411-011: Evolution of the fraction of double-peaks for the \gold\ and \silver\ galaxies in the unbiased sample
elements.sent-2411-518: As our sample is divided by spatial category (\gold, \silver\ and \bronze) in addition to the spectral ones, it is interesting to see if the evolution of the fraction of double-peaks changes in function of the spatial category of the galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-519: In order to observe this evolution, we use the inclusive unbiased double-peak sample $UDP_{I}$ which contains more double-peaks (i.e. more statistics) than the restrictive unbiased double-peak sample $UDP_{R}$.
elements.sent-2411-520: It is important to note here that the inclusive unbiased double-peak sample $UDP_{I}$ only contains \gold\ and \silver\ galaxies.
elements.sent-2411-521: We refer the reader to Sect.~\ref{sec:413 for more details.}
elements.sent-2411-522: To investigate if the fraction of double-peaks of the different categories varies with luminosity, we apply the same procedure as in Sect.~\ref{sec:432 .}
elements.sent-2411-523: We divide our unbiased sample into four \Lya\ luminosity bins with the same number of objects.
elements.sent-2411-524: Figure~\ref{fig:Xdp_per_LumBin shows the fraction of double-peaks for each of the four luminosity bins for $U$ and for the \gold\ and \silver\ categories (in yellow and grey in the figure, respectively).}
elements.sent-2411-525: The fraction of \gold\ double-peaks evolves from around 34\% for the faintest luminosities to 56\% for the brightest bin (41.7 < log(L$_{Ly\alpha}$ [erg s$^{-1}$]) < 43).
elements.sent-2411-526: On the other hand, there is no double-peaked \silver\ objects at the faintest luminosities.
elements.sent-2411-527: Their fraction reaches almost 10\% for intermediate \Lya\ luminosities (41.2 < log(L$_{Ly\alpha}$ [erg s$^{-1}$]) < 41.7) before dropping to 4\% the higher values, due to the significant lack of objects in our parent sample at such luminosities.
elements.sent-2411-528: The fraction of \gold\ double-peaks shows a similar trend as the fraction of the whole sample (black dots in Fig.~\ref{fig:Xdp_per_LumBin ), mainly explained by the high number of \gold\ galaxies populating the sample compared to the \silver\ ones.}
elements.figure-2411-036: 0.6\hsize{!}{\includegraphics{figures/Xdp_per_LumBin_SameNbrObjs_RV_v2.png}} \caption{Fraction of double-peaked LAEs plotted against the logarithmic \Lya\ luminosity. The unbiased sample $U$ has been divided into 4 luminosity bins with the same number of objects (51 or 52). The fraction of double-peaks has been derived in each bin. The results are positioned at the median \Lya\ luminosity of each bin. The black dots represents the total fraction of double-peaks ($N^{UDP_I} / N^{U}$). The $UDP_{I}$ is composed of \gold\ and \silver\ double-peaks. The yellow dots show the fraction of \gold\ double-peaks ($N^{UDP_I \, GOLD} / N^{U}$) among $U$. The fraction of \silver\ double-peaks ($N^{UDP_I \, SILVER} / N^{U}$) is represented by the grey dots. The horizontal black line at the top of the figure shows the size of each \Lya\ luminosity bin.} \label{fig:Xdp_per_LumBin}
elements.sent-2411-529: We also repeat the same procedure to the evolution of the double-peak fraction with redshift.
elements.sent-2411-530: Figure~\ref{fig:Xdp_vs_z shows the evolution of the fraction of double-peaks with redshift for $U$ in black, the \gold\ double-peaks in yellow ($N^{UDP_I \, GOLD / N^{U}$) and the \silver\ double-peaks in grey ($N^{UDP_I \, SILVER / N^{U}$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-531: At low redshift ($z < 3.5$), we see an interesting increase in the double-peak fraction driven by the \silver\ sample.
elements.sent-2411-532: If this trend is confirmed with more data, it could indicate an intrinsic evolution of the LAE population towards cosmic noon, as \silver\ objects are composed of multiple clumps of \Lya\ emission.
elements.sent-2411-533: If such a possible evolution is confirmed, it could make less pertinent the use of the double-peak fraction to probe the opacity of the IGM at this redshift regime.
elements.sent-2411-534: At redshift above 5, no more \silver\ objects are observed, the double-peak fraction being entirely driven by the \gold\ sample.
elements.figure-2411-037: 0.6\hsize{!}{\includegraphics{figures/ZDistribution_FractionDP_complete_only_BinSameNbrObj_8bins_RV_v2.png}} \caption{Fraction of double-peaked LAEs plotted against the redshift. The unbiased sample $U$ has been divided into 8 redshift bins with the same number of objects (25 or 26). The fraction of double-peaks has been derived in each bin. The results are positioned at the median redshift of each bin. The black dots represents the total fraction of double-peaks ($N^{UDP_I} / N^{U}$). The $UDP_{I}$ is composed of \gold\ and \silver\ double-peaks. The yellow dots show the fraction of \gold\ double-peaks ($N^{UDP_I \, GOLD} / N^{U}$) among the unbiased sample $U$. The fraction of \silver\ double-peaks ($N^{UDP_I \, SILVER} / N^{U}$) is represented by the grey dots. The horizontal black line at the top of the figure shows the size of each redshift bin. The black stars surrounded in red represent the mean \Lya\ luminosity of each bin.} \label{fig:Xdp_vs_z}
elements.sent-2411-535: From nebular lines also observed in MUSE spectra, 15 galaxies from the parent sample have a secure systemic redshift.
elements.sent-2411-536: Among them, one galaxy has been studied in detail in \cite{matthee2022 and its systemic redshift has been well constrained (ID 53, last spectrum of Fig.~\ref{fig:zsys_spectra ).}}
elements.sent-2411-537: We refer the reader to Sect.~\ref{sec:53 for more details.}

The shaded coloured areas designate the peaks of the \Lya\ line. The black vertical dashed line is the position of the systemic redshift. First row: Single-peaks. Second, third and fourth rows: Double-peaks. Last row: Triple-peaks.} \label{fig:zsys_spectra}
